steps.title: Spot what 'tell' means in context
steps.content: In "you can tell a man's worth by the number of books he owns," "tell" means assessing or determining someone's character based on observable evidence.

steps.title: Match the meaning to the choice
steps.content:
The underlined word "tell" must mean "assess or determine" — the speaker evaluates a man's worth by observing his books. Substitute each choice:
  • Choice A — Judge: assess or form an opinion about — "you can judge a man's worth by his books" captures the act of evaluating character from evidence. ✓
  • Choice B — Urge: encourage or push — "you can urge a man's worth" is grammatically and logically incoherent in this context. ✗
  • Choice C — Adore: love deeply — adoring a man's worth describes an emotional reaction, not the act of gauging it from books. ✗
  • Choice D — Teach: instruct — books don't teach a man's worth here; the narrator's father uses books as a measuring stick to assess it. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
